title: Matching Strategy to Query Type
concept: A decision framework for picking dense, sparse, hybrid, or multi-query retrieval based on the shape of the incoming question.

exercises.prompt: What technique did we explore to connect LLMs to external databases to prevent them from hallucinating facts?

options.text: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to ground the LLM in external data.
Fine-tuning the LLM on every new document.
Hardcoding response templates for every possible question.

exercises.explain: In the previous unit, we learned that basic retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) fetches documents based on user input to ground the LLM's answer in factual data, reducing hallucinations.

cards.title: One Size Does Not Fit All
cards.body: Not all user questions are built the same! Some look for specific code numbers, some ask for general advice, and others are messy and complex. Using the wrong search strategy leads to poor LLM answers.

cards.title: Query-to-Strategy Mapping
cards.body: Different queries demand different search tools. Sparse retrieval excels at exact keyword matching (like serial numbers), dense retrieval captures conceptual meaning (like synonyms), hybrid combines both, and multi-query rewrites complex questions into multiple search prompts.

cards.title: The Library Assistant Analogy
cards.body: Think of your retrieval systems like finding a book in a massive library. Sparse search is looking up exact words in the index. Dense search is asking a librarian for books about 'sadness'. Hybrid search is using both to find a specific sad poem.

cards.title: Matching in Action
cards.body: If a user searches for 'Error 404 in system log', sparse retrieval is best because '404' is a specific token. If they search for 'ways to improve team happiness', dense retrieval is best because it understands the concept of workplace morale without needing exact words.

cards.title: The Vector Supremacy Myth
cards.body: Many developers assume dense vector search is always superior to old-school keyword search. In reality, dense vectors struggle with exact IDs, product codes, and domain-specific jargon, where sparse keyword search shines.

cards.title: Sparse vs. Dense Queries
cards.body: Match the incoming query's characteristics to the correct tool to get the best context for your LLM.

left.text: Best for: Serial numbers, exact error codes, specific product names.

right.text: Best for: Themes, feelings, synonyms, conceptual questions.

exercises.prompt: A user searches for a specific error code: 'ERR_9921_SYSTEM_FAIL'. Which retrieval strategy is most appropriate?

options.text: Dense retrieval to find similar feeling errors.
Sparse retrieval to match the exact string.
Multi-query expansion to generate synonyms of the code.
No retrieval; let the LLM guess the code.

exercises.explain: Exact codes and serial numbers are highly specific tokens. Sparse search (like BM25) excels at finding these exact matches, whereas dense vectors might dilute them.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Multi-query retrieval is useful when a user's question is poorly phrased or could be asked in multiple different ways.
exercises.explain: Multi-query retrieval works by using an LLM to write multiple variations of the user's question, helping to catch relevant documents that use slightly different phrasing.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the sentence about combining search techniques:
exercises.sentence: When you need to match both specific product serial numbers and general concepts, hybrid retrieval is the best strategy.
exercises.explain: Hybrid retrieval merges the strengths of keyword matching (sparse) and semantic understanding (dense) to deliver the most accurate results for mixed queries.

exercises.prompt: Which of these looks like an AI-generated explanation of query routing?

options.text: If a user asks about pricing, we route them to the SQL database containing our subscription tables. If they ask about feelings, we route to a vector store of self-help guides.
We set up a router using a small classifier model. It reads the query first and decides whether to run a keyword search or a semantic search.
To optimize retrieval efficiency, the system utilizes a decision matrix to route queries of type [User Intent] to the corresponding vector or keyword index.

exercises.explain: Option C is generated by an AI because it uses overly generic, formulaic structures and placeholders like '[insert system name]' instead of demonstrating a natural, concrete scenario.

exercises.prompt: You are designing a customer support assistant. A user submits a complex, multi-part query: 'How do I migrate my legacy SQL database to DynamoDB, and what are the specific error codes for write failures?' Which retrieval setup is best suited to handle this?

options.text: Use sparse-only keyword matching to avoid confusing the database with synonyms.
Use a single dense retrieval step since dense vectors handle complex logic automatically.
Apply Multi-Query expansion to break down the steps, then retrieve using Hybrid (Dense + Sparse) search.
Disable retrieval entirely and rely on the LLM's internal pre-trained knowledge base.

exercises.explain: A complex, multi-layered question requires breaking it down into smaller sub-queries (Multi-Query) to retrieve diverse documents, and then using Hybrid search (Dense + Sparse) to ensure both the specific jargon and the conceptual meaning of those sub-queries are captured.
